title: AI Code Generation: Progress and Limits in Personal Project | Deven Kalra posted on the topic | LinkedIn
author: Deven Kalra
text:
Update from My AI Code Generation Journey Here is a short summary. Full article at https://lnkd.in/gHkQKhtM. I’ve been experimenting heavily with AI-generated code lately. And no, I still refuse to call it “vibe coding”. Credit where it’s due. AI code generation has improved a lot, even in just the last few months. Using AI (mostly Cursor), I worked on a personal project to organize ~30 years of a chaotic collection of photos and videos. It generated professional Python scripts for metadata ingestion, duplicate detection, indexing, file movement, logging, config files, and even a GUI on top. Honestly, the results were impressive. The code worked, iterations did not break existing functionality, and when duplication crept in, refactoring requests actually modularized things correctly. But limits showed up. After a couple of days of continuous generation, changes in one script started breaking others. When explicitly asked, the AI could detect and fix the issues, but each cycle took longer. The real struggle came with testing. I asked for 100% coverage. It produced a large test suite, but the tests would not pass. After several attempts, it effectively gave up with many tests still failing and summarized next steps instead. Still, it delivered: • 235+ test cases • 87% passing for core modules • A complete test framework • Solid documentation So, tongue in cheek, don’t lay off the last few engineers just yet. This is real progress. But replacing experienced developers will take more than better LLMs. We will need fundamentally new techniques. Would love to hear how others are seeing these strengths and limits in practice. Read the full experience on substack at https://lnkd.in/gHkQKhtM
Actually, I think code generators will need to get better in another dimension. I now have multiple projects that AI is unable to look across. I had a project with a very stable data backend. When starting a new project, Claude had no concept that it had worked on that backend and instead proceeded to create a brand new one. After a few hours, I suggested that I had the other data backend available, it whole heartedly agreed that it was quite appropriate and proceeded to augment that one. Just like "attention" (as per the 2017 paper) made LLMs possible and useful, we need "global, and probably selective, attention" to get closer to the human capability.
Thanks for the thoughtful analysis Deven Kalra Can you please write a prequel to this? Talk about what your setup and workflow is like? How often you are updating models etc? To a lot of us the tools and processes are what is leading to productivity gains - the workflow. Learning about what workflows you start with helps us and we can iterate on it as well - esp if you share the repo.
Deven - I had very similar experience of AI based coding for my personal project in financial management. In my view - AI coding systems need to develop techniques for enhanced context/scope of “understanding”. So I agree that they are not yet ready to displace experienced software engineers. Thx for sharing your experiences !
It will be great to see the final product and may be even use it. 🙂
